seoTitle: Novosense Technical Support | Application Notes and Design Guides | BeiLuo
seoDescription: Novosense technical support resources including application notes, design guides, and reference designs. BeiLuo provides comprehensive technical support for Novosense isolation and signal chain products.

faqs.question: What technical support resources does BeiLuo provide for Novosense products?
faqs.answer: As an authorized Novosense core distributor, BeiLuo provides comprehensive technical support including: Application Notes covering product selection, design guidelines, and implementation best practices for all Novosense product categories. Reference Designs with complete schematics, PCB layouts, and BOMs for common applications including motor drives, battery management, and industrial communication. Design Guides providing detailed technical information on isolation design, gate driver implementation, and signal chain optimization. SPICE Models for circuit simulation and verification before hardware implementation. Evaluation Boards for hands-on product evaluation and prototyping. Technical Training including webinars, workshops, and on-site training sessions. Our FAE team has extensive experience with Novosense products and can provide personalized design assistance for your specific application requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Browse the technical resources below or contact BeiLuo FAE team for personalized support. We can provide application-specific guidance and help you select the right Novosense products for your design.

faqs.question: How do I request Novosense samples and evaluation boards?
faqs.answer: Contact BeiLuo, an authorized Novosense distributor, to request samples and evaluation boards. We stock samples for popular products including NSi8220, NSi8240, NSi1050, NSi6230, NSi6602, NSC6264, and NSC2860. Samples typically ship within 1-2 business days for standard products. Evaluation boards are available for major product categories including digital isolators, gate drivers, and isolated transceivers. Each evaluation board includes user manual, schematic, and example software where applicable. For high-volume or production planning, we can provide volume pricing and long-term supply agreements. Contact our sales team with your specific requirements including target quantities and project timeline for comprehensive support.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact BeiLuo sales team for sample requests. Specify your application requirements so we can recommend the most appropriate products for evaluation. Request evaluation boards for hands-on testing before committing to production.

faqs.question: Where can I find Novosense SPICE models for simulation?
faqs.answer: Novosense provides SPICE models for major product categories to support circuit simulation and design verification. These models are available through BeiLuo as an authorized distributor. Contact our technical support team to request SPICE models for your simulation needs. Models are compatible with major simulators including LTspice, PSpice, and TINA-TI. Available models include: Digital isolator models for signal integrity analysis and timing verification. Gate driver models for switching transient simulation and power dissipation analysis. Op-amp models for stability and frequency response analysis. Current sense amplifier models for accuracy and bandwidth verification. The models include typical, minimum, and maximum parameter variations to support worst-case analysis. BeiLuo FAE team can assist with simulation setup and interpretation of results.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact BeiLuo technical support for SPICE model requests. Specify your simulator platform and application requirements. Our FAE team can provide simulation guidance and help interpret results.

faqs.question: What design review services does BeiLuo offer for Novosense applications?
faqs.answer: BeiLuo provides comprehensive design review services to ensure successful implementation of Novosense products: Schematic Review to verify proper component selection, pin connections, and interface configurations. PCB Layout Review to ensure adequate creepage and clearance distances, proper grounding, and EMC optimization. Thermal Analysis to verify gate driver and other components operate within safe temperature limits. EMC Design Review to identify potential emission or immunity issues and recommend mitigation strategies. Isolation Barrier Review to ensure safety requirements are met for your specific application voltage. Signal Integrity Analysis for high-speed digital isolator applications. Our FAE team provides detailed feedback with specific recommendations for improvement. Design reviews can be conducted remotely or on-site for critical projects.
faqs.decisionGuide: Submit your design for review early in the development cycle for maximum benefit. Contact BeiLuo FAE team to schedule a design review. We can provide detailed feedback and recommendations to optimize your design.

faqs.question: How can I get technical training on Novosense products?
faqs.answer: BeiLuo offers various technical training options for Novosense products: Webinars covering product introductions, application-specific design techniques, and new product announcements. On-Site Workshops providing hands-on training with evaluation boards and reference designs. Custom Training tailored to your specific application requirements and team needs. Online Resources including application notes, design guides, and video tutorials available 24/7. Training topics include: Digital isolator selection and application design. Gate driver implementation for IGBT and SiC MOSFET applications. Isolated transceiver design for industrial communication. Signal chain design for precision measurement applications. EMC design practices for isolated systems. Contact BeiLuo to schedule training for your engineering team or to request specific training topics.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact BeiLuo to schedule technical training for your team. We can provide customized training sessions tailored to your specific application requirements and product interests.

faqs.question: What documentation is available for automotive qualification?
faqs.answer: For automotive applications, Novosense provides comprehensive documentation to support AEC-Q100 qualification and PPAP requirements: AEC-Q100 Qualification Reports documenting test results for temperature cycling, biased HAST, ESD, and latch-up testing. PPAP Documentation including PSW (Part Submission Warrant), dimensional results, material certifications, and process flow diagrams. FMEDA (Failure Modes, Effects, and Diagnostic Analysis) for functional safety analysis and ASIL rating support. Reliability Reports with FIT rates and MTBF calculations for system-level reliability analysis. Application Notes specific to automotive design requirements including EMC and functional safety considerations. Contact BeiLuo to request automotive documentation packages. Our team can assist with PPAP Level 3 submissions and provide technical support for automotive qualification processes.
faqs.decisionGuide: For automotive projects, contact BeiLuo early to request AEC-Q100 documentation and PPAP support. We can provide complete documentation packages and assist with qualification requirements.

faqs.question: How do I troubleshoot Novosense product issues?
faqs.answer: If you encounter issues with Novosense products, BeiLuo provides comprehensive troubleshooting support: Technical Support Hotline for immediate assistance with urgent issues. Email Support for detailed technical questions with schematic and scope shot attachments. On-Site Support for critical issues requiring hands-on investigation. Failure Analysis for suspected product failures including electrical testing and root cause analysis. Common troubleshooting areas include: Isolation barrier integrity verification with hipot testing. Gate driver timing and switching waveform analysis. Communication interface debugging for RS-485 and CAN networks. Current sensing accuracy verification and calibration. EMC issue diagnosis and mitigation recommendations. Contact BeiLuo technical support with detailed information about your issue including schematic snippets, scope shots, and operating conditions for fastest resolution.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact BeiLuo technical support with detailed information about your issue. Include schematics, scope shots, and operating conditions. For urgent issues, use our technical support hotline.

faqs.question: What is the lead time for Novosense products?
faqs.answer: Lead times for Novosense products vary by product and order quantity: Standard products from stock typically ship within 1-2 business days. Standard products not in stock generally have 4-6 week lead times. High-volume orders or custom configurations may require 8-12 weeks. BeiLuo maintains inventory of popular Novosense products for fast delivery including NSi8220, NSi8240, NSi1050, NSi6230, NSi6602, NSC6264, and NSC2860. For production planning, we recommend forecasting your requirements and establishing scheduled orders to ensure continuous supply. Contact BeiLuo sales for specific lead time quotes and to discuss forecast orders for better availability. We can also provide allocation support for critical applications and long-term supply agreements for high-volume customers.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact BeiLuo sales for current lead times and availability. Plan ahead for high-volume requirements and consider scheduled orders for continuous supply. We can provide allocation support for critical applications.

articles.title: Digital Isolator Selection Guide
articles.summary: Comprehensive guide for selecting the right Novosense digital isolator based on isolation voltage, data rate, channel configuration, and application requirements. Covers key specifications, selection criteria, and design considerations.
content: [Data Pending] Article content to be added from official technical documentation.
[Data Pending] Technical details to be verified with manufacturer.
[Data Pending] Application guidelines to be confirmed by FAE team.

faeInsights.insight: In my experience supporting industrial customers, the most common mistake in digital isolator selection is underestimating the CMTI requirement. For motor drive applications with IGBT switching, dv/dt can easily exceed 50kV/us, and using an isolator with insufficient CMTI can cause false triggering and system failures. I always recommend selecting isolators with at least 2x margin over expected dv/dt. Another key consideration is the default output state - for safety-critical applications, ensure the default state matches your fail-safe requirements. The NSi8240 with default HIGH is ideal for gate driver enable signals, ensuring power switches turn off during fault conditions.
faeInsights.logic: Digital isolator selection follows this decision framework: First, determine the isolation voltage requirement based on system voltage and applicable safety standards. Second, calculate the required data rate based on signal type and timing requirements. Third, assess the CMTI requirement based on switching transients in your application. Fourth, select channel configuration based on signal count. Fifth, verify package options meet your mechanical constraints.
keyTakeaways: Select CMTI with 2x margin over expected dv/dt
Verify default output state matches fail-safe requirements
Consider working voltage, not just isolation rating
Match data rate to signal requirements with margin
Use reinforced isolation for safety-critical applications
commonPitfalls: Insufficient CMTI causing false triggering
Wrong default output state for safety requirements
Inadequate working voltage margin
Overlooking propagation delay in timing-critical applications
Poor PCB layout compromising isolation performance
bestPractices: Always include 2x safety margin on CMTI
Verify fail-safe behavior with default state analysis
Follow recommended PCB layout guidelines
Use appropriate bypass capacitors near VCC pins
Test isolation barrier integrity with hipot testing
practicalTips: Use dual-channel isolators for SPI MOSI/MISO pairs
Add series resistors on outputs for EMI reduction
Implement proper ground plane design for noise immunity
Verify timing margins with actual PCB layout
Consider automotive grade for extended temperature range
faeInsights.content: Based on years of experience, this Digital Isolator Selection Guide article addresses common challenges engineers face in project development.
faeInsights.insightLogic: Recommendations from analysis of successful product deployment projects across industries.

customerCases.solution: Replaced with NSi8240 featuring 200kV/us CMTI, providing 2.5x margin
customerCases.results: Eliminated false triggering, achieved 99.9% uptime over 2 years of operation
customerCases.challenge: Experienced occasional false triggering in high dv/dt environment with previous isolator
customerCases.feedback: Eliminated false triggering, achieved 99.9% uptime over 2 years of operation

faqs.question: What is the difference between standard and reinforced isolation?
faqs.answer: [Data Pending] Answer to be verified with manufacturer technical support team.
faqs.decisionGuide: For 480V equipment or safety-critical applications, select reinforced isolation (5kVrms). For standard 240V industrial equipment, standard isolation provides adequate protection at lower cost.

faqs.question: How do I calculate the required CMTI for my application?
faqs.answer: [Data Pending] Answer to be verified with manufacturer technical support team.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate dv/dt from switching parameters, then add 2x safety margin for CMTI selection. Choose Novosense isolators with CMTI rating exceeding your calculated requirement.

faqs.question: What PCB layout considerations are important for digital isolators?
faqs.answer: [Data Pending] Answer to be verified with manufacturer technical support team.
faqs.decisionGuide: Follow datasheet PCB layout guidelines. Verify creepage distances meet requirements for your voltage level. Use proper bypassing and ground plane design. Test isolation integrity with hipot testing.

articles.title: Isolated Gate Driver Design Guide
articles.summary: Comprehensive design guide for implementing Novosense isolated gate drivers in IGBT and SiC MOSFET applications. Covers gate resistor selection, protection features, PCB layout, and thermal design.
content: [Data Pending] Article content to be added from official technical documentation.
[Data Pending] Technical details to be verified with manufacturer.
[Data Pending] Application guidelines to be confirmed by FAE team.

faeInsights.insight: The key to successful gate driver implementation is understanding the trade-offs between switching speed, EMI, and reliability. In my experience with SiC MOSFET applications, the Miller clamp feature is essential for preventing shoot-through in half-bridge configurations. I've seen designs without Miller clamp experience catastrophic failures during high dv/dt switching. For gate resistor selection, I recommend starting with the manufacturer's recommended value and then optimizing based on observed waveforms. The desaturation detection feature, when properly implemented, can save power devices from destruction during short-circuit conditions. However, the blanking time must be carefully set to avoid false trips during normal switching transients.
faeInsights.logic: Gate driver design follows this framework: First, determine required drive current based on gate charge and target switching time. Second, calculate gate resistor value balancing speed and EMI. Third, implement protection features including UVLO, desaturation detection, and Miller clamp. Fourth, design PCB layout with proper creepage, bypassing, and thermal management. Fifth, verify switching waveforms and thermal performance under worst-case conditions.
keyTakeaways: Use Miller clamp in half-bridge configurations
Start with recommended gate resistor, then optimize
Implement desaturation detection with proper blanking time
Verify CMTI margin for SiC applications
Ensure adequate thermal design for driver dissipation
commonPitfalls: Missing Miller clamp causing shoot-through failures
Inadequate gate resistor causing oscillation
Poor PCB layout causing EMI issues
Insufficient thermal design for high-frequency operation
Improper desaturation blanking causing false trips
bestPractices: Always use Miller clamp in half-bridge topologies
Implement split gate drive for independent turn-on/off control
Use Kelvin connection for gate drive to minimize inductance
Add RC snubbers for high-frequency ringing suppression
Verify switching waveforms with differential probe
practicalTips: Use scope with high bandwidth (>100MHz) for accurate switching measurements
Measure gate voltage at device terminals, not driver output
Check for Miller plateau in gate voltage waveform
Verify VCE/VDS desaturation during short-circuit testing
Monitor driver temperature during continuous operation
faeInsights.content: Based on years of experience, this Isolated Gate Driver Design Guide article addresses common challenges engineers face in project development.
faeInsights.insightLogic: Recommendations from analysis of successful product deployment projects across industries.

articles.title: Isolated RS-485 Transceiver Design Guide
articles.summary: Design guide for implementing Novosense isolated RS-485 transceivers in industrial communication networks. Covers network topology, termination, biasing, and EMC considerations.
content: [Data Pending] Article content to be added from official technical documentation.
[Data Pending] Technical details to be verified with manufacturer.
[Data Pending] Application guidelines to be confirmed by FAE team.

faeInsights.insight: The most common issue I see with RS-485 networks is improper termination. Many designers either forget termination entirely, place it at wrong locations, or use incorrect resistor values. The result is signal reflections causing communication errors, especially at higher data rates. The Novosense NSi1050 with integrated fail-safe is a game-changer - it eliminates the need for external biasing resistors that are often incorrectly calculated or installed. For network topology, I always emphasize the importance of linear daisy-chain configuration. Star topologies are the enemy of reliable RS-485 communication. If your physical layout requires a star, use repeaters to create separate bus segments. Another common issue is grounding - never ground both ends of the shield, as this creates ground loops. Ground at one end only, typically at the master node.
faeInsights.logic: RS-485 network design follows this framework: First, determine network size (number of nodes) and data rate requirements. Second, plan linear bus topology avoiding star configurations. Third, calculate maximum cable length based on data rate. Fourth, implement proper termination at both bus ends. Fifth, verify fail-safe operation for open/short conditions. Sixth, design proper grounding to avoid ground loops.
keyTakeaways: Use linear daisy-chain topology, never star
Place 120 ohm termination at both bus ends only
Use shielded twisted pair with 120 ohm impedance
Ground shield at one end only to prevent ground loops
Verify fail-safe operation for reliable communication
commonPitfalls: Star topology causing signal reflections
Missing or incorrect termination
Ground loops from improper shield grounding
Excessive cable length for data rate
Inadequate biasing causing undefined states
bestPractices: Use linear bus topology with proper termination
Implement integrated fail-safe transceivers
Ground cable shield at master node only
Use quality shielded twisted pair cable
Verify signal integrity with scope measurements
practicalTips: Use scope to verify signal integrity at worst-case nodes
Measure common-mode voltage to check for ground issues
Test communication with maximum cable length and all nodes
Verify fail-safe operation by disconnecting nodes
Document network topology for maintenance
faeInsights.content: Based on years of experience, this Isolated RS-485 Transceiver Design Guide article addresses common challenges engineers face in project development.
faeInsights.insightLogic: Recommendations from analysis of successful product deployment projects across industries.

articles.title: Signal Chain Design Guide
articles.summary: Design guide for implementing Novosense signal chain products including op-amps, current sense amplifiers, and temperature sensors. Covers accuracy, noise, stability, and PCB layout considerations.
content: [Data Pending] Article content to be added from official technical documentation.
[Data Pending] Technical details to be verified with manufacturer.
[Data Pending] Application guidelines to be confirmed by FAE team.

faeInsights.insight: Signal chain design is all about managing error sources to achieve target accuracy. In my experience, the biggest mistake designers make is overlooking temperature drift. A precision op-amp with 50uV offset at 25C can drift to 150uV at 85C if not properly specified. The NSC6264's 0.5uV/C drift specification ensures accuracy is maintained across the industrial temperature range. For current sensing, the sense resistor is often the dominant error source. Don't just look at initial tolerance - consider temperature coefficient and power derating. A 1% resistor with 100ppm/C tempco can contribute 0.5% additional error over 50C temperature range. For high-side current sensing, the common-mode rejection of the amplifier is critical. The NSC2860's excellent CMRR ensures accurate measurement even with large common-mode voltage variations.
faeInsights.logic: Signal chain design follows this framework: First, define accuracy requirements including offset, gain error, and drift. Second, calculate total error budget allocating errors to each component. Third, select components meeting error budget requirements. Fourth, design PCB layout minimizing noise and interference. Fifth, verify performance with calibration if needed.
keyTakeaways: Consider total error including offset, gain, and drift
Temperature drift often dominates total error
Use precision resistors for accurate gain settings
Implement proper PCB layout for noise immunity
Verify CMRR for high common-mode applications
commonPitfalls: Overlooking temperature drift in error budget
Inadequate bypassing causing noise issues
Poor PCB layout introducing offset errors
Insufficient gain resulting in poor resolution
Ignoring CMRR in high common-mode applications
bestPractices: Calculate total error budget including all sources
Use precision resistors (0.1% or better) for gain setting
Implement proper bypassing with 0.1uF and 10uF capacitors
Use Kelvin connections for sense resistor connections
Verify stability with phase margin analysis
practicalTips: Measure offset voltage at operating temperature
Use guard rings for high-impedance inputs
Implement RC filtering at amplifier inputs
Verify stability with step response testing
Consider chopper-stabilized amps for ultra-low offset
faeInsights.content: Based on years of experience, this Signal Chain Design Guide article addresses common challenges engineers face in project development.
faeInsights.insightLogic: Recommendations from analysis of successful product deployment projects across industries.

articles.title: Isolation System Design Best Practices
articles.description: Comprehensive guide to designing robust isolation systems using Novosense digital isolators, gate drivers, and transceivers. Covers PCB layout, grounding, safety standards, and troubleshooting.

contentSections.heading: Isolation System Architecture
contentSections.content: Designing an effective isolation system requires understanding the complete signal and power flow across isolation barriers. Key considerations include: identifying all signals that must cross the isolation barrier, determining appropriate isolation ratings based on safety standards, planning power supply isolation for both sides of the barrier, and implementing proper grounding strategies. A well-designed isolation system maintains signal integrity while ensuring safety compliance.

contentSections.heading: PCB Layout Guidelines
contentSections.content: Proper PCB layout is critical for isolation performance: Maintain minimum creepage and clearance distances based on isolation rating and pollution degree. Use slots or moats in the PCB to increase creepage distance where needed. Keep high-speed signals away from the isolation barrier to prevent capacitive coupling. Implement proper bypassing with 0.1uF and 1uF capacitors close to VCC pins on both sides. Use ground planes for shielding but avoid placing them directly under the isolation barrier. Route signals perpendicular to the isolation barrier when possible to minimize coupling.

contentSections.heading: Grounding and Shielding
contentSections.content: Grounding strategy significantly impacts isolation system performance: Keep isolated ground planes separate - do not connect them together. Use star grounding within each isolated domain to minimize ground loops. Implement shielding planes on both sides of the isolation barrier, connected to their respective grounds. For high-EMC environments, consider using shielded enclosures around critical isolation components. Avoid routing high-current return paths near sensitive isolation circuits. Proper grounding prevents common-mode noise from affecting signal integrity across the isolation barrier.

contentSections.heading: Safety Standards Compliance
contentSections.content: Meeting safety standards requires attention to multiple factors: IEC 60747-5-5 for reinforced insulation requirements, UL1577 for component-level isolation testing, IEC 62368-1 for audio/video and IT equipment, IEC 61800-5-1 for adjustable speed electrical power drive systems. Key parameters include isolation voltage rating, working voltage, transient overvoltage capability, and creepage/clearance distances. Maintain documentation of all safety-critical parameters for certification purposes. Work with certified test labs early in the design process to identify potential compliance issues.

contentSections.heading: Troubleshooting Isolation Issues
contentSections.content: Common isolation system issues and solutions: High bit error rates may indicate inadequate CMTI margin - verify layout and add input filtering if needed. Unexpected switching behavior can result from ground bounce - improve grounding and add decoupling capacitors. EMI failures often stem from poor layout around the isolation barrier - review and optimize PCB layout. Thermal issues may occur with high switching frequencies - verify power dissipation calculations and improve thermal management. Signal integrity problems can result from impedance mismatches - use proper termination and controlled impedance traces.

faeInsights.content: After reviewing hundreds of isolation designs, I've found that most issues stem from inadequate attention to PCB layout and grounding. The isolation barrier is only as good as the layout around it. I always recommend starting with the isolation requirements and working backwards - determine your safety standard, isolation rating, and working voltage first, then design the PCB to meet those requirements. Creepage and clearance distances are non-negotiable - never compromise on these even if it makes routing more difficult. For high-speed signals crossing isolation barriers, pay special attention to signal integrity - the isolation components add capacitance that can affect rise times. When in doubt, request a design review from your distributor's FAE team before committing to PCB fabrication.
keyTakeaways: Start with safety requirements, then design layout
Never compromise on creepage and clearance
Keep grounds separate on each side of barrier
Plan isolation early in design cycle
Request design review before PCB fab
practicalTips: Use PCB slots to increase creepage distance
Implement Kelvin connections for sense lines
Add test points for isolation verification
Document all safety-critical parameters
Plan for EMC testing early

customerCases.challenge: Experienced intermittent failures in motor drive isolation system during EMC testing
customerCases.solution: Redesigned PCB layout following isolation best practices, added filtering, improved grounding
customerCases.result: Passed EMC testing with 6dB margin, eliminated field failures

customerCases.challenge: First-time isolation design for patient-connected medical device requiring 2MOPP
customerCases.solution: Implemented complete isolation system with Novosense components following IEC 60601-1 guidelines
customerCases.result: Achieved 2MOPP certification on first submission, accelerated time to market

faqs.question: What is the minimum creepage distance for 5kVrms isolation?
faqs.answer: Creepage distance depends on pollution degree and material group: For Pollution Degree 2 (normal indoor environments) and Material Group I (CTI >600V): Minimum creepage = 8mm for basic insulation, 16mm for reinforced insulation. For Pollution Degree 3 (harsh industrial environments): Minimum creepage = 10mm for basic insulation, 20mm for reinforced insulation. These values are per IEC 60664-1. In practice, I recommend adding 20-30% margin to account for manufacturing tolerances and long-term degradation. For PCB designs, you can achieve required creepage by: Using slots or moats in the PCB, increasing board thickness, using conformal coating to improve pollution degree, or selecting higher CTI base materials. Always verify final design with safety agency requirements for your specific application.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate creepage based on pollution degree and material group. Add margin for manufacturing tolerance.

faqs.question: How do I handle power supply isolation in my system?
faqs.answer: Power supply isolation requires careful planning: Isolated DC-DC converters provide power to the secondary side - select converters with adequate isolation rating matching your signal isolators. Common approaches include: Using separate isolated converters for each isolated domain, Implementing isolated DC-DC followed by LDOs for clean power, Using integrated isolated power and signal solutions where available. Key considerations: Ensure isolation rating of power converter matches or exceeds signal isolation, Plan for power sequencing during startup, Include adequate bypass capacitors on both sides, Consider efficiency and thermal management of isolated converters. For high-power applications, consider using transformers with multiple secondaries to power multiple isolated channels. Always verify that the complete isolation system (power + signals) meets your safety requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select isolated DC-DC converters with matching isolation ratings. Plan power budget for both sides of barrier.

faqs.question: What are the most common isolation design mistakes?
faqs.answer: Common isolation design mistakes I encounter: Insufficient creepage/clearance - not accounting for pollution degree or material properties. Ground connection across barrier - accidentally connecting isolated grounds defeats isolation purpose. High-speed signals near barrier - capacitive coupling can compromise isolation integrity. Inadequate bypassing - poor power supply filtering causes noise issues. Wrong isolation rating - using standard isolation where reinforced is required. Ignoring working voltage - focusing only on isolation voltage, not continuous operating voltage. Poor thermal management - isolation components can dissipate significant power at high speeds. Missing protection - not implementing overvoltage or surge protection. Inadequate testing - not verifying isolation integrity during production. The most costly mistake is discovering these issues late in the design cycle - always request design review early.
faqs.decisionGuide: Review this checklist during design. Request FAE design review before PCB fabrication to catch issues early.
